attributes.stem:
An 82-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a one-month history of lightheadedness when standing up from sitting or lying. She has fallen twice in the past month without injury. She has a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and osteoarthritis. Her medications include perindopril, amlodipine, moxonidine, metformin, and paracetamol. She has a well balanced diet and drinks 2.5-3L oral fluids per day.

On examination, she appears well. Her BMI is within normal limits. Her blood pressure is 142/85 mmHg when lying and drops to 108/65 mmHg after standing for three minutes, with an associated increase in pulse rate from 68/min to 84/min. Her respiratory rate is 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. The remainder of her examination is unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptomatic postural hypotension, demonstrated by a significant drop in blood pressure upon standing along with symptoms of pre-syncope and falls. The presence of multiple antihypertensive medications, particularly moxonidine, suggests an iatrogenic cause.

Ceasing moxonidine is the most appropriate first step as it is a centrally-acting antihypertensive commonly associated with postural hypotension, particularly in elderly patients. Medication review and cessation of contributing medications should always be the first intervention before considering additional measures.

Starting fludrocortisone, a mineralocorticoid that increases sodium retention and plasma volume, would be premature before addressing potentially causative medications. It can also worsen supine hypertension and cause fluid overload.

Midodrine is an alpha-1 agonist that can be useful in treating orthostatic hypotension, but like fludrocortisone, it should only be considered after simpler interventions have been tried.

While increased fluid intake is a reasonable non-pharmacological measure for orthostatic hypotension, it is unlikely to be sufficient given the severity of the blood pressure drop and presence of falls, and the fact that the patient already has adequate oral fluids intake.

Compression stockings can help manage orthostatic hypotension by preventing venous pooling, but they should be considered as an adjunctive measure after addressing medication-related causes.

The management of orthostatic hypotension should follow a stepwise approach, starting with identification and modification of contributing factors (especially medications), followed by non-pharmacological measures, and only then considering pharmacological interventions if symptoms persist in consultation with specialist input.
learningPoints: Moxonidine is a centrally-acting antihypertensive that commonly causes postural hypotension, particularly in elderly patients.
Medication review is the first step in managing postural hypotension before considering additional medications.
Postural hypotension is diagnosed by a drop in systolic blood pressure of ≥20 mmHg or diastolic blood pressure of ≥10 mmHg within 3 minutes of standing.
references: Ringer M, Lappin SL. Orthostatic Hypotension. In: StatPearls [online]. 2023. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448192/

attributes.name: Graduating
